title: Real-Time Tilt Undersampling Optimization during Electron Tomography of Beam Sensitive Samples using Golden Ratio Scanning and RECAST3D
author: Craig, Timothy M.
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description: Electron tomography is invaluable for characterizing the 3D structure and morphology of nanomaterials. However, this technique requires long exposure times and high electron dosages, which induce damage in sensitive samples and, consequently, causes artefacts in the 3D reconstruction. Researchers typically limit the damage by reducing beam exposure to mitigate these artefacts. One such approach, tilt undersampling, reduces exposure by limiting the number of acquired projections. However, reducing the number of projections also induces reconstruction artefacts due to limited sampling. Hence, obtaining a reconstruction of beam-sensitive samples requires simultaneous optimization of the number of projections to limit artefacts from undersampling and beam exposure. Conventionally, this is achieved by comparing multiple reconstructions acquired with a variable number of projections. Unfortunately, this process is time-consuming, requiring repeated acquisition and post-processing. Furthermore, each acquisition must be performed on a new sample due to damage occurring. Hence, reconstructions are not directly comparable. Herein, a streamlined protocol is proposed using golden ratio scanning and quasi-3D reconstruction to estimate the optimum number of projections in real-time during a single acquisition. The procedure was tested using simulated and realistic nanoparticles. Finally, two beam-sensitive metal-organic framework complexes were reconstructed using this new protocol.

keywords: Metal-Organic Frameworks, Electron Tomography, Beam-Damage, Real-Time Tomography, Nanomaterials
